coredata.dc:title: High rate, long cycle life potassium metal anode based on multifunctional nanodot additives
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yan
Han, Weibo
Wang, Ling
Liu, Shan
coredata.dc:description: Potassium mental batteries (PMBS) have received keen concern in energy storage and supply, because of its high energy density and abundant resources. Whereas, the high reactivity of potassium metal resulted in side reactions and dendrites growth, which can significantly affect commercial application of the batteries. In this work, the carbon dots (CDs) are introduced as an electrolyte additive to inhibit side reactions and dendrites growth. The surface of ultrasmall CDs is modified by electronegative functional groups, which make it provide abundant active sites for ion transfer and show affinity for K+, which can adsorb K+ to achieve uniform deposition. The K||K symmetric cells with CDs can cycle steadily more than 1000 h, and the limited current density can reach 5.5 mA cm−2. The work is simple and easy to implement, with good universality, providing an effective solution strategy for the application of high rate, long-life potassium metal batteries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dendrite-free
Potassium metal anode
Carbon dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of the CDs preparation process. (b) Digital photos of prepared CDs powder. (c) The FTIR pattern of the prepared CDs. (d) The XRD pattern of the prepared CDs.

(a) Optical picture of electrolyte of KPF6 in DEC: EC with various concentrations CDs. (b) The liquid Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy of the electrolyte containing CDs before and after K metal soaking. (c) Optical photograph of K metal soaking in electrolyte containing CDs. (d) Tafel curves of the K||K symmetrical cell without and with CDs additives. (e) and (f) Nucleation overpotential of K||Cu half-cell without and with CDs additives. (g) Cycling stability of K||K symmetric cells without and with CDs additives at 0.1 mA cm−2.

The scanning electron microscope images of K metal anode in the K||Cu half-cell without (a-b) and with (d-e) CDs additives. The scanning electron microscope images of K metal anode in the K||K Symmetrical cells after cycle 100 h at 0.1 mA cm−2 without (c) and with (f) CDs additives.

(a) limited current density test of K||K symmetric cells with CDs; (b) rate performance of K||K symmetric cells with CDs; (c) CA curves of K||K symmetric cells with CDs; (d) LSV curves of K||Al cells without and with CDs additives at a scan rate of 0.5mV s−1. (e) Nyquist plots of K||K cells without and with CDs after 1st and 20th cycles; (f) CV curves of K||Cu cells at a scan rate of 0.5 mV s−1.

(a) Atomic model of the synthesized CDs. (b) Binding energies of K atoms with the CDs by density functional theory (DFT) calculations. (c) Potassium dendrite growth with and without CDs additives by Finite element simulation, respectively. (d) Schematic diagrams of potassium metal anode deposition without or with CDs additives in the electrolyte.

head.ce:title: High rate, long cycle life potassium metal anode based on multifunctional nanodot additives

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, North China University of Science and Technology, Tangshan 063009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: North China University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, North China University of Science and Technology, Tangshan 063009, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Potassium mental batteries (PMBS) have received keen concern in energy storage and supply, because of its high energy density and abundant resources. Whereas, the high reactivity of potassium metal resulted in side reactions and dendrites growth, which can significantly affect commercial application of the batteries. In this work, the carbon dots (CDs) are introduced as an electrolyte additive to inhibit side reactions and dendrites growth. The surface of ultrasmall CDs is modified by electronegative functional groups, which make it provide abundant active sites for ion transfer and show affinity for K+, which can adsorb K+ to achieve uniform deposition. The K||K symmetric cells with CDs can cycle steadily more than 1000 h, and the limited current density can reach 5.5 mA cm−2. The work is simple and easy to implement, with good universality, providing an effective solution strategy for the application of high rate, long-life potassium metal batteries.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Potassium metal batteries (PMBs) have become a potential generation of energy storage and power supply system because of their high energy density, relatively abundant resources and low cost (Zhang et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2019; Sun et al., 2023; Hosaka et al., 2020; He and Nazar, 2017; Shadike et al., 2017). However, there are some obstacles in the practical application of potassium metal batteries, such as K dendrite growth during cycling, high reactivity and volume expansion of potassium metal anode, and the larger ionic radius of K+ in electrolyte, which resulting in reduced coulomb efficiency and limited cycle life (Li et al., 2021; Fang et al., 2019; Li et al., 2022; Hu et al., 2024; Han and Meng, 2023). In particular, substantial research efforts are focused on problems of side reactions and dendrite growth.

Researchers have proposed some strategies to above problems, such as, electrolyte modification or additives (Fang et al., 2020); solid electrolyte (Yi et al., 2024), composite electrode (Fang et al., 2019) and alloy anode (Luo et al., 2022). Interface engineering is one of the key initiatives in the exploration of high-performance batteries, including natural and artificial SEI. Artificial SEI can improve ionic conductivity and inhibit dendrite growth by optimizing composition (Zhao, 2022; Jiang et al., 2023). However regulating electrolyte chemistry through the selection of salts, solvents, and additives is very effective because it directly affects the properties of the SEI layer and improves deposition behavior (Jiang et al., 2024). Adding additives to the electrolyte is a simple and economical method to realize interface stability for K metal anode (Wang et al., 2022; You et al., 2024). The carbon dots (CDs) are a kind of ultra-small carbon material whose three-dimensional size is less than 10 nm (Hou et al., 2017). Compared with traditional carbon materials, it has advantages of stable chemical properties, rich surface functional groups, low toxicity, and superior dispersity (Wang et al., 2022; Li et al., 2021; Ye et al., 2021). Moreover, the surface structure of carbon dots can be regulated and functionalized by adjusting the number and type of precursors, so as to adjust its physical and chemical properties (Hong et al., 2019). Therefore, CDs have the potential to be used as an additive to effectively regulate the deposition behavior of potassium metal anode (Shaker et al., 2023; Hou et al., 2015).

Herein, we designed carbon dots (CDs) as an electrolyte additive to carbonate electrolyte to inhibit the growth of dendrites and side reaction. The synthesized CDs contains rich polar functional groups and exhibits electronegativity, which can be used as active sites. During electroplating process, CDs can adsorb the surrounding K+ to achieve co-deposition and form a dense deposition layer, thus improving the performance of the batteries. Therefore, the cells with CDs exhibit superior electrochemical performance. The symmetric cells of K||K with designed CDs can cycle stably exceed 1000 h and the critical current density can reach 5.5 mA cm−2.

The synthesis mechanism of carbon dots is shown in Fig. 1
a. Firstly, in an alkaline environment, aldehydes undergo aldol condensation continually, including α-C dehydrogenation, carbonyl addition, dehydration to produce unsaturated aldehydes. Then, unsaturated aldehydes occur a series of condensation reactions to produce a series of functional groups, branch chains and tiny carbon clusters. The polymer chains and carbon clusters should be coiled and entangled, and gather on the surface of CDs as previous literature (Li et al., 2021). In the preparation of CDs, the reaction of NaOH with acetaldehyde produces brown sticky product. It is neutralized by adding hydrochloric acid and then dried to produce CDs solid powder ( Fig. S1 ). Fig. 1 b is an optical picture of CDs powder after grinding, presenting as brownish yellow, as previous literature reported (Li et al., 2021).

The optical photo of the electrolyte with different concentrations CDs is shown in Fig. 2
a. It was found that the synthesized CDs have good dispersion in esters solvents because CDs have abundant polar groups, which is an important factor as an additive (Li et al., 2021; Fu et al., 2020; Tu et al., 2023). Ultra-small CDs with excellent dispersion can effectively adsorb reactants and inhibit agglomeration due to their nucleation sites (Wang et al., 2022; Shaker et al., 2023). Furthermore, it indicated that K metal anode have good chemical stability with CDs (Fig. 2 b and c). The fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) have no significant change, after soaked potassium metal block in electrolyte containing CDs for a week, the result is consistent with those reported in the literature (You et al., 2024).

In order to investigate the role of CDs additives, a variety of kinetics tests were also performed. The exchange current density was measured by Tafel curve in Fig. 2 d. The exchange current density of the cell using electrolyte containing CDs is 1.824 × 10−4 A cm−2, nearly an order of magnitude higher than the electrolyte without additives (2.341 × 10−5 A cm−2), which should be attributed to the fact that the addition of CDs is conducive to the charge transfer in electrolyte and interfacial diffusion kinetics of the K metal anode have been improved (Sun et al., 2023). Subsequently, we also use the Arrhenius equation to calculate the activation energy of K+ diffusion in SEI layer. The diffusion activation energy of the battery without and with CDs is 40.41 and 29.18 kJmol−1, respectively ( Fig. S5 ). The results demonstrate that the introduction of CDs accelerates the rate of K+ passing through the SEI layer (Li, 2025); which was consistent with those of Tafel test.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis (XPS) was carried out to ascertain the components of the interface layer of anode, the characteristic peaks in the K2p spectrum appearing at 292.5 and 295.8 eV which may be due to the co-deposition of CDs and K+ at the anode during the cycle. The weak peaks in the O1s spectra attribute to CO and CO, in contrast, the interface CO and CO are less after the addition of CDs, indicating that the solvent decomposition of the electrolyte is reduced ( Fig. S8 ). In order to further prove the mechanism of CDs promoting deposition uniformly, the deposition morphology of K||Cu half cells with and without CDs deposited on Cu foil were charactered by scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Fig. 3 ). The deposited surface of the K metal anode containing CDs is smooth and flat, due to the electronegativity of CDs, K+ and CDs achieve co-deposition, the deposited layer is relatively dense (Fig. 3 d and e). But there are many dendrites and pores on the surface of the cells without CDs under the same conditions (Fig. 3a and b). The “tip effect” occurs and dendrites are formed (Li et al., 2021) due to the uneven electric field distribution and inhomogeneous deposition of K metal anode.

The SEM images of the K||K symmetrical cells without and with CDs additive after cycling 100 h were also charactered, as shown in Fig. 3 c and f. The surface of K metal anode in the electrolyte without CDs has numerous cracks and holes. The potassium dendrite and dead K are produced, leading to forming a porous and irregular K deposition layer. On the contrary, the deposited layer after adding CDs to the electrolytes has little dendritic K and dead K during cycling, and the electrode is flat, which suggests that CDs can regulate the homogeneous deposition of potassium ions and agree with their electrochemical performance.

The limited current density test of K||K symmetric cells were carried out, as shown in Fig. 4
a, the critical current density can reach 5.5 mA cm−2 of the cell with CDs, which is better than most previously reported. (Feng et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2024; Li et al., 2023) However, the limited current density is only 3.5mA cm−2 for the cell without CDs ( Fig. S9 ), due to the continuous growth of the dendrites and the side reaction increases. The limited current density test of the cells was further explored with and without CDs at different temperatures (40 ℃). It is also found that the cell with CDs has superior performance in Fig. S10 .

To further characterize the impact of the CDs, we also evaluated the rate performance of symmetrical cells. The current density can reach 4.5 mA cm−2 of the symmetric cells with CDs, which is better than the cells without CDs (3.0 mA cm−2) in (Figs. 4 b and
S11 ). The K+ diffusion process in different electrolyte was verified by Chronoamperometry measurement (CA) (Fig. 4 c). For the symmetric cell containing CDs, it showed a stable and smooth current after 120 s, indicating that the presence of CDs can achieve uniform deposition. However, for the cell without CDs, the ion

flux distribution is inhomogeneous and the dendrite growth seriously ( Fig. S12
). In addition, the electrolyte with CDs also showed a higher electrochemical window of 4.8 V and indicated improvement of electrolyte stability. The same is true in DME ether electrolytes ( Figs. S13 and 4 d).

The EIS tests were used to further explore the properties of the electrolyte (Fig. 4 e). The charge transfer resistance in the electrolyte without CDs was greater than that with CDs. The impedance of the cells without CDs increased more than the cells with CDs after 20 cycles, this may be the continuous consumption of electrolyte leading to the instability of the SEI layer formed, which increases the internal resistance. The interface reaction of K metal anode was also charactered by the cyclic voltammetry curve (CV) with a scanning speed of 0.5mV s−1 (Fig. 4 f). According to the literature, the cathodic peak accurs around 0.5 V vs K||K+ corresponding to the reduction of solvent (Ma et al., 2022). The reduction potentials of the cells with CDs additives were slightly higher than the cells without CDs. As discussed above, CDs provide more active sites for disordered K+ in the electrolyte, which promotes uniform nucleation of K+ and reduce the occurrence of interface polarization and side reactions caused by uneven deposition. The cells without CDs showed a broad and sharp cathodic peak which indicated a more serious reaction.

There are abundant polar functional groups on the CDs surface, which have a strong affinity for K+ (Fig. 5
a and b), and the binding energy for OH, COOH, CO are −0.45, −0.41 and −0.24 a. u. respectively (Dong et al., 2019). The electronegativity of polar functional groups makes CDs provide more active sites for the absorption of K+, the strong affinity between K+ and functional groups can be used to adsorb K+ to achieve uniformity deposition [2, 25]. For further analyzing the deposition behavior of K+ on the anode, dendrite growth on the surface of K was simulated by finite element simulation (Fig. 5 c) (Ye et al., 2021). For the K metal anode without CDs, the ion deposition is not uniform due to the irregular protrusions on the surface. In the long deposition and stripping will lead to the growth of dendrites. However, for the K metal anode with CDs, the diffusion and flow rate of K+ is accelerated and more evenly. CDs contains rich functional groups and it is electronegative, which will provide more nucleation sites and adsorb K+, inhibit the growth of dendrites and make the deposit layer dense (Fig. 5 d).

In summary, we propose to use CDs as an electrolyte additive to suppress dendritic growth, the synthesized CDs has polar functional groups and good dispersion in the electrolyte. CDs with abundant sites can promote the adsorption and diffusion of K+, which can induce deposit uniformly and inhibit dendrite growth. The electrochemical performance of the cells with CDs were improved, the K||K symmetric cells can stably cycle more than 1000 h and the limited current density can reach 5.5 mA cm−2. This work provides a simple and feasible method for regulating the dendrite growth of the K metal anode, which has a great significance for the industrial application of potassium metal batteries.

Yan Liu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Weibo Han: Methodology, Investigation. Ling Wang: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Shan Liu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Natural Science Foundation of China (52371187), The Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei Basic Research Cooperation Special Project (Grant No. B2024209048), The Central Government Guides Local Science and Technology Development Funds of Hebei Province (226Z4403G).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ces.2025.121388.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
